frictions.text: Four of the original antithesis pairings (values-generate-disagreement vs values-representable; no-false-fact vs jettison-modeled; nobody-wants-rigor vs built-for-readers; over-determined vs many-grounds-native) are challenge-ANSWER pairs whose members can both be true, not rivals. The grammar has no 'responds-to' relation to express that one claim answers another without rivaling it, so these pairings had to be dropped entirely; the response-ness is now only implicit in the limb-level antitheses.

demoted.reason: Redundant path: n048 grounds n046 which reaches n035 via n036; the direct n048->n035 shortcut duplicates that path.

Re-route: n049 (public record raises the discourse floor) is a mechanism of n046 (change comes through the not-yet-committed audience), not a direct ground of the root conclusion. Restores the mirrored-limb structure and keeps n002 off the hub line.
Re-route: n054 (essayists prove fair surveys are valuable; a map democratizes them) supports n035 (a viable audience and production route exists), the audience limb, not the root directly.

Re-route: n055 (a faithful map is irreducibly complex; explicit beats implicit) answers the complexity/form critique and is the s6 rival to n008 -- it supports the form limb n027, not the root directly.

meta.question: Will a structured, collaborative argument-mapping platform meaningfully improve collective reasoning on contested questions -- or are attempts to 'solve debate' doomed not to work?

phrasings.text: A structured, collaborative argument-mapping platform will NOT meaningfully improve real-world reasoning or debate on contested questions.
A faithful, collaborative argument map meaningfully improves collective reasoning on contested questions -- as a durable shared artifact for third parties, not a debate-winning tool for the arguers.

Whether an argument-mapping platform 'works' depends on what is demanded of it: it cannot RESOLVE genuinely value-laden disagreement, but it can faithfully MAP it -- so the dispute over such platforms is substantially about the GOAL, not the mechanism.

titles.text: Depends what you demand of it

phrasings.text: The argument-map form presumes a premise-to-conclusion skeleton with locatable false links -- a shape that real disagreement does not have.

titles.text: Maps assume a shape arguments lack

phrasings.text: Real arguments almost never decompose into clean premises and a conclusion where a skeptic can point to the one false premise or invalid link.

titles.text: No clean premises to point at

phrasings.text: Real disagreements rarely hinge on a checkable false fact or a nameable fallacy -- and when one is pointed out, people simply drop it and keep arguing on other grounds.

titles.text: Rarely a false fact or fallacy

phrasings.text: When a panel of experts who disagreed sharply about AI risk tried to explain their disagreement, the closest anyone came was that some weight a theoretical intelligence-explosion argument heavily while others distrust theoretical arguments generally -- no false fact and no named fallacy, just different weighing.

Adding more premises and links to an argument map makes it worse, not better: it recasts inherently probabilistic reasoning as logically necessary syllogisms and trains readers to hunt for a single link to refute.
Even a completed argument map settles only the truth of atomic premises, which is not where real disagreements are decided.
The load-bearing work in a real dispute is quantifying magnitudes and weighing them against each other.

A published worked example: even granting 'lockdowns hurt the economy' as true, concluding 'lockdowns were bad' still requires quantifying the economic harm, all the other harms, the benefits, comparing them for a specific proposal, and a theory of interpersonal utility.
The deepest disagreements are generated by differing values and ethical frameworks (e.g. 'this policy violates rights regardless of any cost-benefit'), which no amount of factual mapping can resolve.
People hold positions on contested questions through many weakly-held considerations, not one decisive crux.
Refuting any single consideration rarely changes anyone's overall position.

titles.text: Flipping one point moves no one

phrasings.text: The decisive action in a disagreement happens at the level of worldviews and other high-level generators; surface-level claims -- where argument maps operate -- are the wrong altitude.
Argument-mapping is not a practically viable route to improving real-world debate.
Formal argument-mapping is an inefficient way to change anyone's mind in a live dispute.
Almost no one actually wants to argue more rigorously: people who argue about contested questions are there to win and to express, not to update.
Two thousand years of philosophy and rhetoric have not 'solved debate', which is evidence that the problem is not fixable by a new tool.
The conceded limitations of argument-mapping platforms -- granted even by their builders -- bear directly against the claim that such platforms work.
If 'working' means making the disagreeing parties agree, argument maps do not work: they produce a shared map, not consensus.
A collaborative platform's internal rating rules can only measure agreement with its own crowd, so on a biased majority they reward the bias.
Correcting a biased crowd requires external ground truth, which is scarcest on exactly the most contested questions.

titles.text: Anchors are scarce where needed most

phrasings.text: The assessment research behind one argument-mapping project publicly retired its own agreement-based reputation rule for burying the sharpest raters, found its discrimination scoring inverted on a biased crowd, and found its calibration had to decline when external anchors did not span the scale.
Statistical dispersion in ratings is a low-reliability signal of where a map is genuinely contested, so contestedness has to be read from the map's structure instead.
Whether real human communities will build, adopt, or trust collaborative argument maps is unproven; the demonstrations so far come from AI-agent panels.
An argument map does not have to assume the clean logical form the critique attacks: a grammar of graded support, rival positive claims, and joint grounds captures how real arguments actually behave.

titles.text: A richer grammar fits real argument

phrasings.text: An argument map need not force premise-to-conclusion form: support can be graded rather than binary, opposition can be a rival positive claim rather than a negation, and grounds can be marked as supporting only jointly.
A graph holds an over-determined position natively: a claim can carry many independent grounds with support apportioned across them, so no single decisive crux is required.

In a graded, support-only map, a refuted ground is demoted while its dependent survives on its remaining grounds -- 'drop the refuted point and continue' is the modeled normal case, not a failure of the form.
The proper job of a map on the hard parts of a dispute -- the weighing and the values -- is to locate and exhibit them, not to resolve them.

titles.text: Locate the crux, don't resolve it

phrasings.text: The goal of mapping a dispute is not to resolve the weighing but to LOCATE it: to make explicit where the weight sits and how wide the reasonable range is.

On a mapped and blind-panel-assessed build of a genuinely contested scientific question (the origin of a pandemic virus), the honest verdict node 'the causal question is currently unresolved' scored high -- the assessed map declined to manufacture a false resolution.

Value claims can be represented first-class and separately: typed as prescriptions, barred from grounding factual claims, and rated on endorsement rather than truth -- so value-cruxes appear on the map AS values rather than being smuggled into factual claims.
Argument maps have a viable audience and production route that does not depend on changing the arguers' minds.

titles.text: A viable route and audience exist

phrasings.text: An argument map's audience is third parties -- readers and future deciders consulting a durable shared artifact -- not the two arguers in a live dispute; changing the arguers' minds is the wrong success measure.
AI agents can build, maintain, and rate argument maps at scale, removing the authorship cost that sank earlier mapping efforts.

Three differently-shaped contested questions -- a pandemic virus's origin, collider black-hole safety, and the healthfulness of eggs -- have each been built and blind-panel-assessed end-to-end into argument graphs by AI agents at low cost.
The inference from 'unsolved for two thousand years' to 'unsolvable' fails, because the enabling conditions for argument-mapping are genuinely new.
'Unsolved for two thousand years' does not imply unsolvable now: cheap AI authorship and rating, reputation-weighting, and enforced-blind assessment are conditions no previous attempt had.

Encyclopedias, prediction markets, and systematic review each languished until the right medium and incentive structure arrived -- longstanding failure before a workable form is the norm for such tools, not evidence of impossibility.

The strongest critiques of argument-mapping target RESOLUTION -- making the parties agree -- which mappers concede they do not deliver, while the mapping claim is about REPRESENTATION; the two sides are arguing about what the tool is FOR, not about whether its mechanism functions.
The dispute over whether argument-mapping 'works' is itself over-determined and partly value-laden, so by the critique's own analysis it will not be resolved -- it can only be located and mapped.
A tool that faithfully maps a disagreement -- making its value-cruxes explicit and openly unresolved -- is worth building, even if it never makes the parties agree.

titles.text: A faithful map is worth it

phrasings.text: A debate tool is only worth building if it actually changes minds or produces agreement; a map that leaves everyone where they started is not worth the effort.

A public argument map improves collective understanding mainly through people who have not yet taken a side: new readers form their views in a world where weak arguments are already visibly weak, even if committed arguers never change their minds.

Nearly all of Wikipedia's facts existed in print before Wikipedia; what ended casual factual disputes was easy, universal access to one trusted repository, which made checkable falsehoods embarrassing to repeat.

Like facts before Wikipedia, the points made in public arguments already exist -- scattered across essays, studies, and threads -- with no single trusted place where their strength has been fairly assessed.

Refuting a false fact rarely changes a committed believer's mind, but a trusted public record of which arguments are known to be weak makes them costly to repeat, and so raises the quality of public discourse over time.

No one can hold a complete map of a complicated dispute in their head -- every claim, every connection, and an accurate sense of how strongly each point is really supported -- so an explicit map adds information even for the most capable readers.
A complicated dispute contains more claims, connections, and strength-judgments than a person can hold in their head at once, so an explicit map adds information even for the most capable readers.

titles.text: No one holds the whole map

phrasings.text: After the 2024 Rootclaim debate on COVID origins, the losing side said the live format gave a major advantage to the debater who had memorized more -- memory limits shaping the outcome even of an elite, high-stakes debate.

titles.text: Memory limits bind even elite debate

phrasings.text: The gap between what most people find convincing and what is true is a limit on every way of arguing -- essays, expert panels, peer review -- not a special defect of argument maps; a format can at best measure that gap and manage it.

titles.text: A limit shared by every format

demoted.reason: Split: bundled three supply channels (point-droppers + side-defenders + AI authorship). AI authorship is already carried by n037; the other two are now separate atomic claims (n053 rated a low 1.97, read as overclaimed 'full coverage').

phrasings.text: People who will not sustain a long argument still reliably drop single points, and partisans arrive when their side is visibly losing; collecting and organizing those scattered contributions, plus AI authorship, yields full coverage without users who want to argue rigorously.

The most trusted essayists already prove that a comprehensive, fair survey of a hard question is valuable; a collaborative map aims to make that kind of survey available for every question, without depending on the rare writer able and willing to produce one.

titles.text: Surveys should not require rare talent

phrasings.text: A faithful map of a complicated dispute cannot be simpler than the dispute itself; the real choice is between an explicit map that can be shared, checked, and read a piece at a time, and an implicit map hidden in one person's head.

demoted.reason: Split: bundled two distinct concessions -- (1) a complete map is hard to take in at once, and (2) heavy map use tempts treating one weak link as defeating an over-determined argument. The second is a rival to advocate n030 (graded support survives a dropped link), so splitting surfaces a real crux.

phrasings.text: A complete argument map is genuinely hard for a person to take in at once, and heavy use of maps can tempt readers to treat one weak link as defeating a whole over-determined argument.

titles.text: Maps are hard to take in

phrasings.text: People who will not sustain a full argument still reliably contribute single points -- a correction, a source, a single objection -- which a collaborative map can collect and organize into coverage.
Partisans reliably arrive to defend their side when it is visibly losing, supplying the strongest available counter-arguments without anyone needing to sustain a rigorous debate.
A complete argument map of a complicated dispute is genuinely hard for a person to take in at once.

titles.text: Maps are hard to take in

phrasings.text: Heavy reliance on argument maps can tempt readers to treat one weak or refuted link as defeating an otherwise over-determined argument.
